item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of the entity that lures in child predators but with a different target audience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason, the one thing that breaks suspension of disbelief for me - and which I suspect might even raise red flags if I got caught by this thing in universe - is the bit about the forum posts being uniformly positive. No "this author is a hack and you should feel bad for liking her" troll posts? No "albums x, y, and z are wonderful but albums q and r don't really measure up" vs. "dude q and r are his best albums, what are you smoking" arguments? No "I'm a fan but such and such a subplot in the second movie made me deeply uncomfortable and not in a good way" analysis?

Uniformly positive posts aren't critical acclaim, they're sycophancy. It makes it come across less as "you're wildly successful in an alternate life where you followed your dreams" and more as "this is a conspiracy to fuck with you." Which may be closer to the truth, but if it is, it should do a better job of hiding it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps elaborating on the suicide rate. perhaps relating it to a memetic effect? I enjoy the concept, but there are still quite a few flaws that need to be fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the idea, the execution is mostly great. Only a few problems: some examples could be useful, with comments from the subject; the ending feels a little abrupt. It would be interesting if there was a hint to what happened to the researcher, maybe his forum account is still active, on other versions of the website. Still, definitely an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's no way I should love this as much as I do. It's nothing special, kind of breaks down at points, and yet I adore it.

+1, would +2 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i liked it. apart from the depression it just makes people happy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty nice idea, and… it just starts to fall flat by the end. The test log in particular feels phoned in, even though it's also a good idea. Too rushed, not believable enough.

I might upvote anyway, but… I'll have to think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a guy who sometimes thinks he should be a writer or musicion, this is actually creepy as balls. I actually liked it with the [REDACTED] bit at the end. People can say what they want, but the bit you put after it did constrain what got redacted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of a website that subtly mocks you with a look at a version of you more successful and popular than you yourself ever will be, but that idea doesn't really go far enough. I have the same complaints as everyone else: the creep is rather tacked-on, and the suicide rate is unrealistic. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to imagine that if in-universe Dr. Gears ever came across this SCP, he'd find that he was the beloved admin of a horror wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least this one makes you depressed for a reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Website of Erised!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes sense, I didn't thought of that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the depression and suicide seems very tacked-on and nonsensical. 30% is incredibly high. If you really want to keep that in, I would elaborate on the effects of the website more (which I would say you should do anyway) and reduce that rate greatly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking that it would lead to a social network page for [famous authors name], the non-famous private citizen who's leading an aggravatingly rich, fulfilling life as a result of being successful at [thing that [famous author] failed at before he said "Fuck it, lets try writing for a living"]. Or maybe a wiki page for [famous authors name] the wildly successful businessman/porn star/diplomat. The basic idea being "The grass really is greener and you'd be a lot happier if you had done X but it's too late now." Everyone has doubts/questions about the life choices they've made and (in my head, at least) this anomaly works by leveraging those.

-edit-

Latest edits do it for me, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the core idea, but the 30% suicide rate is dumb. It seems like your intention was that victims became depressed because they realized what they could've accomplished, rather than the depression being an anomalous effect in and of itself. However, that isn't consistent with such a ridiculously high suicide rate, since realistically it should be about a tenth of what it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go right ahead; I wouldn't make suggestions like that if I didn't want them to be used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, it's entirely suitable for an SCP, it's just that the description makes it sound like there's non-anomalous difficulties keeping the Foundation from tracking down the server and/or owner of the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hardly see how that analogy suggests this is unsuitable for an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Execution needs work (in particular, the suicide thing is pretty stupid, in my opinion), but it's an interesting idea. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great idea! Given my luck, all the people on my version of the forums would be crazy stalkers.

Only question is what happens if someone who is a famous author goes on the website?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like your idea. I'd like to add it, although I figure I should get your permission first.

Also, I agree the ending is pretty weak, so I've changed it so nothing was found at all. Sometimes less is more, I guess. I hope that's better, but if not let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP, however, as several people have pointed out, the sites (specifically the store and maybe the wiki) make it so that the subject could just republish. I would personally edit it to say something like, "the wiki will only give short summaries of each written work" or "everything in the store appears to be permanently out of stock." (You'd have to change the tones for those, of course.) This is just a suggestion. Also, I feel that the bit at the end not only was unnecessary, but actually took away from the quality of the article. In any case, I think this is a good SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…An analogous situation with the postal system would have letters to a certain postal adress simply vanishing into thin air before they got to the destination, with the reply letters appearing out of thin air into postal trucks…

We…actually have a good deal of that in other articles. Not saying that makes it more credible, just saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attempts to locate the owners of the site or its servers have so far proven futile.

This would, in and of itself, be anomalous. An analogous situation with the postal system would have letters to a certain postal adress simply vanishing into thin air before they got to the destination, with the reply letters appearing out of thin air into postal trucks.

Something which wouldn't break the suspension of disbelief of more tech-savvy readers would be something like:

The domain ████████████.com is not registered by anyone, and when usually accessed gives the normal "DNS lookup failed" error. However, if viewed by an individual with a history of creative aspirations, SCP-1228 will appear as the page of the viewer’s “Official Fan Club.” Any software used to monitor Internet connections crashes when applied to connections to SCP-1228, so its IP address is unknown.

With something like that, the containment procedure would change to searching the Internet for references to the domain name, then erasing those references and giving amnesiacs to anyone involved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The suicide bit is dumb, but I think the idea is neat. Would upvote if this gets shored up a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dislike it, and there is no reason for books to begin dancing meringue with site personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shortly afterwards, Dr. █████████ and the books began to [DATA EXPUNGED]

I like it, but then pissed me off. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me too much of the MP3 player that populates itself with music by the listener, complete with biography and their eventual demise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. I really enjoyed this, in spite of whatever errors it has. It's a really nice idea. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy fuck, Misery flashbacks.

Okay, yeah, I can buy that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a stretch to imagine the house had the books there.

After all, she's your biggest fan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously, though, if I went to this website and bought a book of poetry by an alternate-universe me, I wouldn't be depressed. I'd be DELIGHTED. I like to write poetry but don't want to do it as a career, but it'd be nice to have, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But the last paragraph with the novels is still there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) If we know the URL and the IP address, how can we not locate the servers?

2) If the store sells copies of the author's hypothetical books, what's to stop the author from simply buying a copy and submitting it for legit publication?

3) How can we not trace credit card transactions made through the store?

This is less a quality SCP and less the author moaning about how he'd be rich and famous and swimming in groupies if he'd written that book instead of going to college.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point. I'll make it so only merchandise is sold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Containment procedures do nothing for me, and seem ridiculous, though I don't have the technical background to know for sure. In any case the execution is very dry and uninteresting. Not too fond of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the conversation log come across a little…corny? Not sure if that's the right word. Anyway, that's a small thing. The big thing is:

Store: A store exists for the purchase of the subject’s work (books, films, albums, video games, etc.) and merchandise pertaining to it, such as T-shirts. Within ██ hours of making a purchase, an unmarked package will appear at the subject’s address containing the product(s) in question.

Unless I'm reading this wrong, this gives me all the benefits of being a productive author without the drawback of having to sit my ass down at the keyboard and bang my head against a wall to get the words out. For reasons that i hope are obvious, that doesn't fit with the 30% suicides idea. I see what you're going for here, but I really think that having access to the works, rather than just reviews of and praise for works that I never wrote, doesn't…err…work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see what stops people from trying to plagerize their own work, at least before the Foundation discovered it. Are the novels consistent with the writing styles of aspiring-writer victims? Do they use ideas the victims have thought of for a plot?

No vote for now. Feels like it needs to be fleshed out more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My guess? They realize what they COULD have been, or could have done.

If you had proof/an idea that, had you followed those fleeting, creative urges you had, you'd have become famous, you'd have positively altered the world… I can see it being depressing to people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The execution isn't very good. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this! However, the ending feels kind of meh. The books [DATA EXPUNGED] was interesting, but it just felt a bit narmy to me. Also, why wouldn't they have monitored the doctor during this time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This… is… not.. bad,

but:

After viewing SCP-1228, test subjects will report feelings of depression, with thirty percent attempting suicide shortly afterwards.

Why? There needs to be something more written on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've made the ending a little bit less vague. I always hate when [DATA EXPUNGED] is used as a cop-out, so I feel pretty bad about doing the same thing. Hopefully that's better. I'll make things more clear about the packages too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting concept, but the execution is ho-hum. The Foundation says they can't find the owners of the site, but you think they'd be more focused on the servers. A 30% suicide rate is ridiculously high, I'd understand many instances of clinical depression, but a 30% would definitely be an anomalous feature (and an unnecessary one in terms of writing). Has the Foundation put any extra work into finding the source of these mysterious packages, like observing when they "appeared"? I think this article would also greatly benefit from a better description at the end instead of just [DATA EXPUNGED], which right now just seems to be a cop-out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unnecessary bolding in the containment procedures, several tonal issues, and it's essentially a website what makes you depressed.

I don't hate it, though, so no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. Not sure how I feel about the depression thing, though?

The private messages thing is an interesting direction but the pay off is kinda phoned in.
